data.content:
Field-Guide-to-SA-Offshore-Marine-Invertebrates
A field guide to South African offshore marine invertebrates, containing information on species identification, distribution, and taxonomy.<SEP>The field guide document about South African offshore marine invertebrates, containing descriptions and images on various pages.

Starfishes of the Atlantic Volume 3
A book by Clark AM and Downey ME (1992) covering starfishes of the Atlantic, referenced in the field guide.

The Echinoderms of Southern Africa
A book by Clark AM and Courtman-Stock J (1976) covering echinoderms of Southern Africa, referenced in the field guide.

Echinodermata
Echinodermata is a phylum of marine invertebrates. This taxonomic group includes a diverse array of organisms such as starfish, sea urchins, and related species. All described starfish species belong to this phylum, and specific examples within Echinodermata include *Luidia sarsii africana*, *Chondraster elattosis*, *Spoladaster veneris*, and *Poraniopsis echinaster*. 

Echinoderms are characterized by distinctive anatomical features, including a stellate (star-shaped) body, a reticular skeleton, raised spines, tube feet, and a white madreporite. As marine invertebrates, members of this phylum are found in various oceanic environments and are often mentioned in scientific descriptions and imagery contexts. The phylum represents a major group within the animal kingdom, encompassing a wide range of species that share these common morphological traits.

P. echinaster
A species of starfish described in the field guide, with short-armed stellate body form, reticular skeleton, raised spines on aboral surface, two rows of tube feet, and white madreporite. Colour ranges from deep orange to red or pure white. Found in the South Atlantic including West Coast of South Africa.<SEP>A species mentioned in the document, with arms clearly defined and having size averaging 50-160 mm diameter.

Lophaster quadrispinus
A similar species to P. echinaster, with many dense raised tubercles on the aboral surface.<SEP>A similar species described in the document, having many dense raised tubercles on the aboral surface.

Diplopteraster multipes
A similar species to P. echinaster, more cushion-like with arms not as clearly defined.<SEP>A similar species described in the document, more cushion-like with arms not as clearly defined as P. echinaster.

data.entity_name: South Atlantic West Coast of South Africa
data.content:
South Atlantic West Coast of South Africa
Geographic distribution of P. echinaster, including the South Atlantic Ocean and the west coast of South Africa.

Reticular Skeleton
A spiky skeleton with soft tissue covering, characteristic of P. echinaster.<SEP>Reticular skeleton is an anatomical feature of Poraniopsis echinaster.<SEP>A spiky skeleton with soft tissue covering, characteristic of the described organism.

Aboral Surface Spines
Distinct raised spines covering the aboral surface of P. echinaster, 1-4 mm in length.

Tube Feet
The Thick Fleshy Starfish has double rows of tube feet.<SEP>Two rows of tube feet present in P. echinaster.<SEP>Double rows of tube feet on the Inflexible Rigid Star.<SEP>Double rows of tube feet present.<SEP>Two rows of tube feet present on the organism.

Madreporite
The madreporite is distinct in the Thick Fleshy Starfish species.<SEP>White in colour, located off-centre halfway to the base of arms in P. echinaster.<SEP>Distinct madreporite of the Inflexible Rigid Star.<SEP>Distinct madreporite present.<SEP>White madreporite located offcentre halfway to base of arms.

Base Arm Spines
Strong spines along the base of arms in P. echinaster.

Distinguishing Features
A section of the field guide describing the distinguishing features of P. echinaster, including body form, skeleton, spines, tube feet, and madreporite.<SEP>A section on page 429 of the Field Guide to SA Offshore Marine Invertebrates, describing distinguishing features of marine invertebrates, as indicated by the neighbor text adjacent to the image.<SEP>Section on page 429 describing the distinguishing features of the organism.<SEP>A section on page 429 describing the distinguishing anatomical features of an invertebrate, including arms, spines, and central disc.<SEP>A section heading in the document that describes the distinguishing features of the organism belonging to phylum Echinodermata.

Colour
A section of the field guide describing the colour of P. echinaster, ranging from deep orange to red or pure white, with light red to yellowish white spines and pale oral surface.<SEP>A section heading in the document about the colour of the organism; no colour description is provided in the input text.

Size
A section of the field guide describing the size of P. echinaster, averaging 50 to 160 mm in diameter.<SEP>The Size section describes the dimensions of the featured marine invertebrate.

Distribution
A section of the field guide describing the distribution of P. echinaster in the South Atlantic including the West Coast of South Africa.<SEP>The Distribution section provides geographic and depth range information for the featured marine invertebrate.

Similar Species
A section of the field guide listing species similar to P. echinaster, such as Lophaster quadrispinus and Diplopteraster multipes.<SEP>Similar Species is a section in the field guide that likely discusses species similar to the featured invertebrate.

References
A section of the field guide listing references, including Starfishes of the Atlantic (Volume 3) and The Echinoderms of Southern Africa.<SEP>A section within the document that contains references and distinguishing features of an organism.

Stellate Body Form
A short-armed, stellate body form characteristic of P. echinaster.

Arm Rigidity
The arms of P. echinaster are fairly rigid.

Arm Curling
Behavior of P. echinaster where the ends of arms often turn upwards or curl inwards.

Oral Surface
The oral surface of the Thick Fleshy Starfish is smooth and pale yellow in colour, with tube feet.<SEP>The oral surface of P. echinaster is pale in colour.<SEP>Smooth oral surface of the Inflexible Rigid Star.<SEP>Smooth oral surface.

Chondraster Elattosis
Chondraster Elattosis is a species of starfish that does not inflate and is leathery. It is a South African endemic found on the West and South Coasts of South Africa at depths from 400 to 1000+ meters.

Spoladaster Brachyactis
Spoladaster Brachyactis is a species of starfish that is pentagonal, cushion-like, often inflated, with papillae on the aboral surface and a smooth ventral side. It is found on the West and South Coasts of South Africa at depths from 40 to 205+ meters, up to 160 mm diameter, with speckled brilliant orange aboral surface and pale cream oral surface.<SEP>Spoladaster brachyactis is a starfish species that inflates and is not as leathery compared with Spoladaster veneris.

Spoladaster Veneris
Spoladaster Veneris is a similar species to Chondraster Elattosis; it inflates and is less leathery compared to Chondraster Elattosis.<SEP>Spoladaster veneris is a starfish species with code name SpoBra, belonging to Phylum Echinodermata, Class Asteroidea, Order Valvatida, Family Poraniidae, Genus Spoladaster, and Species veneris. Its common name is Inflated Star.

Phylum Echinodermata
Phylum Echinodermata is the taxonomic phylum to which the starfish species Chondraster Elattosis and Spoladaster Brachyactis belong.<SEP>A taxonomic phylum of marine invertebrates characterized by radial symmetry and a water vascular system, presented on page 430 of the document.

Cushion-Like Body
The cushion-like body is a pentagonal, inflated shape characteristic of Spoladaster Brachyactis when landed.<SEP>The organism has a cushion-like body that appears inflated when landed.<SEP>The body is cushion-like and often inflated when landed, slowly deflating out of water.

Papillae on Aboral Surface
Numerous papillae coat the aboral surface of Spoladaster Brachyactis.

Ventral Smooth With Fine Lines
The ventral side of Spoladaster Brachyactis is smooth with fine lines.

Inflation Behavior
The behavior of inflating when landed is characteristic of Spoladaster Brachyactis; Chondraster Elattosis does not inflate.

Deflation Behavior
The behavior of slowly deflating over time out of water is observed in Spoladaster Brachyactis.

data.entity_name: West And South Coasts Of South Africa
data.content:
West And South Coasts Of South Africa
The geographic distribution of both Chondraster Elattosis and Spoladaster Brachyactis along the West and South Coasts of South Africa.<SEP>The geographic range of the invertebrate includes the west and south coasts of South Africa.

Depth Range 400 To 1000+ M
The depth range for Chondraster Elattosis is from 400 to 1000+ meters.

Depth Range 40 To 205+ M
The depth range for Spoladaster Brachyactis is from 40 to 205+ meters.

Document Doc Sa Invertebrates P0429 0432
The document containing field guide information for marine invertebrates, pages 429-432.

Section Distribution
The distribution section of the document providing range information for species.

Section Distinguishing Features
The distinguishing features section describing morphological characteristics.

Section Colour
The colour section describing the coloration of the species.

Section Size
The size section providing measurements.

Section Similar Species
The similar species section comparing related species.

Section References
The references section listing cited works.

Reference Clark And Courtman-Stock 1976
Reference: Clark AM and Courtman-Stock J. 1976. The Echinoderms of Southern Africa. British Museum (Natural History): London. pp. 73-74.

Reference Clark And Downey 1992 Volume 3
Reference: Clark AM and Downey ME. 1992. Starfishes of the Atlantic (Volume 3). Chapman and Hall: London. pp. 202-204 and 222-224.

230 Mm Diameter
The measurement of 230 mm diameter, likely referring to the size of Chondraster Elattosis as indicated on page 430 of the document.

Dr C. Mah
Dr C. Mah is a scientist from the Smithsonian who confirmed the species identification in June 2015.<SEP>Dr C. Mah is a scientist who confirmed the species identification of the echinoderm depicted in the image in June 2015.

Smithsonian
Smithsonian is an institution in Washington, D.C., where Dr C. Mah works.<SEP>Smithsonian is an institution located in Washington, with which Dr C. Mah is affiliated.

Reference Clark And Downey 1992 (Pages 202-204)
Reference: Clark AM and Downey ME. 1992. Starfishes of the Atlantic (Volume 3). Chapman and Hall: London. pp. 202-204.

Reference Clark And Downey 1992 (Pages 222-224)
Reference: Clark AM and Downey ME. 1992. Starfishes of the Atlantic (Volume 3). Chapman and Hall: London. pp. 222-224.

Luidia Africana
Luidia africana is a starfish species with distinguishing features: arms that break off easily from the central disc, distinct spines on the aboral margin, long flexible flattened strap-like arms, usually five arms. Colour brown to dark pink. Size up to 150 mm diameter. Distribution: Southern African endemic, West and South Coasts of South Africa to Port Elizabeth, 54 m to 360+ m depth.<SEP>A distinct species or taxonomic entity mentioned as similar to Luidia sarsii africana, indicating potential synonymy.

Astropecten Polyacanthus
Astropecten polyacanthus is a similar species to Luidia africana, with less flattened and broader arms.

Astropecten Exilis
Astropecten exilis is a similar species to Luidia africana, with less flattened and broader arms.

Thick Fleshy Starfish
An unidentified starfish species described on page 430 with inflexible rigid star, thick solid spongy disc, pentagonal shape, indistinct marginal plates, distinct madreporite, fine raised bumps (sheaths of adambulacral spines) forming rows along arms, no spines, double rows of tube feet, no marginal plates visible, distinct patterning on aboral surface when brooding. Colour bright pink to orange on aboral, pale yellow on oral. Size up to 230 mm diameter. Distribution: South African endemic, West and South Coasts of South Africa.

Central Disc
The central disc of Luidia africana is the region from which arms extend, and arms break off easily from it.<SEP>The central body part of the starfish from which the arms break off easily.<SEP>Central disc from which arms break off easily.<SEP>The central disc is the central body part from which arms break off easily.

Arms
Arms of Luidia africana are long, flexible, flattened, tapering, strap-like, usually five in number, and break off easily from the central disc.<SEP>Long, flexible, flattened, tapering, strap-like arms that usually break off from central disc easily. Usually five arms.<SEP>The arms are long, flexible, flattened, tapering, strap-like, usually five in number, and break off easily from the central disc.<SEP>The starfish has arms with rows of adambulacral spine sheaths.<SEP>Fairly rigid arms with ends often turning upwards or curling inwards, with strong spines along the base.<SEP>Arms are appendages of the species; for P. echinaster they are clearly defined, while for Diplopteraster multipes they are less clearly defined and the species is more cushion-like.

Aboral Margin Edge
The aboral margin edge of Luidia africana has distinct spines protruding from it.<SEP>Edge of the aboral margin where distinct spines protrude.<SEP>The edge on the aboral side of the invertebrate where distinct spines protrude.

Spines
Spines are present on the aboral margin edge of Luidia africana.<SEP>Distinct spines protruding from the aboral margin edge.<SEP>Distinct spines that protrude from the aboral margin edge.

Adambulacral Spines Sheaths
Fine raised bumps (sheaths of adambulacral spines) form distinct rows along each arm in the Thick Fleshy Starfish.

Patterning On Aboral Surface
The aboral surface of the Thick Fleshy Starfish has distinct patterning when brooding, as shown in the third image.

Third Image
The third image in the document shows the distinct patterning on the aboral surface of the Thick Fleshy Starfish when brooding.<SEP>An image referenced in the distinguishing features text on page 430 of the field guide, showing the aboral surface patterning of the Inflexible Rigid Star during brooding.

Distribution Of Luidia Africana
Luidia africana is a Southern African endemic, found on the West and South Coasts of South Africa to Port Elizabeth, at depths from 54 m to over 360 m.

Distribution Of Thick Fleshy Starfish
The Thick Fleshy Starfish is a South African endemic, found on the West and South Coasts of South Africa.

data.entity_name: Field Guide To Sa Offshore Marine Invertebrates
data.content:
Field Guide To Sa Offshore Marine Invertebrates
The document titled "Field Guide to SA Offshore Marine Invertebrates" from which the text is extracted, containing descriptions of marine invertebrates.<SEP>Source document title of the PDF from which the table is extracted.<SEP>A field guide document on South African offshore marine invertebrates, containing images and descriptive text.

Distinguishing Features Section
A section of the document that describes distinguishing features of starfish species, present on both page 429 and page 430.<SEP>A section in the document on page 430 describing distinguishing features of Phylum Echinodermata.<SEP>A section on page 430 of the field guide, containing neighbor text describing distinguishing features of the Inflexible Rigid Star.<SEP>A section describing the distinguishing features of the starfish.<SEP>A section of the document on page 431 that describes the distinguishing morphological features of an echinoderm.<SEP>Section of the field guide describing distinguishing anatomical features of the organism.<SEP>A section in the field guide that describes the distinguishing anatomical features of Phylum Echinodermata.

Colour Section
A section of the document that describes the colour of starfish species, present on both page 429 and page 430.<SEP>A section describing the colour of the starfish.<SEP>A section in the field guide that describes the colour of the organism.

Size Section
A section of the document that describes the size of starfish species, present on both page 429 and page 430.

Distribution Section
A section of the document that describes the distribution of starfish species, present on both page 429 and page 430.<SEP>A section in the document that describes the distribution of the species.

Similar Species Section
A section of the document on page 429 that compares Luidia africana to similar species.<SEP>A section in the document that compares similar species to the main species.

References Section
The References Section is a part of a field guide on invertebrates, specifically associated with document identifier doc_sa_invertebrates_p0429_0432. This section appears across pages 429, 430, and 431, and is explicitly labeled "references" on page 429. It contains reference information for an image of starfish, including a citation to the work "Starfishes of the Atlantic." Additionally, the section includes species identification notes and image content analysis metadata. Its neighbor text leads to a description of distinguishing features of the Phylum Echinodermata. The section serves as a bibliographic listing and also provides contextual metadata for identifying and differentiating the depicted organisms.

Phylum Section
A section of the document on page 429 that states the phylum Echinodermata.

Clark Am And Downey Me 1992
A reference document: Clark AM and Downey ME. 1992. Starfishes of the Atlantic (Volume 3). Chapman and Hall: London. p. 20.

Marginal Plates
Marginal plates are indistinct or not visible in the Thick Fleshy Starfish, present along the arms but not apparent.<SEP>Indistinct marginal plates on the Inflexible Rigid Star, not visible on the aboral or oral surface.<SEP>Indistinct marginal plates, not visible.

Spongy Disc
The spongy disc is the thick, solid, spongy central part of the Thick Fleshy Starfish, pentagonal in shape.

Aboral Surface
The aboral surface of the Thick Fleshy Starfish is smooth, bright pink to orange in colour, and can have distinct patterning when brooding.<SEP>Smooth aboral surface of the Inflexible Rigid Star, with distinct patterning when brooding.<SEP>Smooth aboral surface with distinct patterning when brooding.<SEP>The upper surface of the organism, covered with papillae.<SEP>The aboral surface is covered with papillae.<SEP>The aboral surface is the upper surface of a starfish-like organism, mentioned in the description of Lophaster quadrispinus as having many dense raised tubercles.

Port Elizabeth
Port Elizabeth is a city on the South Coast of South Africa, within the distribution range of Luidia africana.

West Coast Of South Africa
The West Coast of South Africa is a coastal region along the Atlantic Ocean, part of the distribution range for both Luidia africana and the Thick Fleshy Starfish.<SEP>The West Coast of South Africa is a distribution area for Poraniopsis echinaster.

South Coast Of South Africa
The South Coast of South Africa is a coastal region along the Indian Ocean, part of the distribution range for both Luidia africana and the Thick Fleshy Starfish.

South Africa
South Africa is the country where the described starfish species are endemic to its coasts.

Page 429
Page 429 of the field guide contains the description of Luidia africana, including distinguishing features, colour, size, distribution, similar species, references, and phylum.<SEP>The 429th page of the field guide, containing a distinguishing features description and an image of an invertebrate.<SEP>Page 429 of the document Field-Guide-to-SA-Offshore-Marine-Invertebrates_web-full-version_compressed.pdf, containing the References Section and the image.

Page 430
Page 430 of the field guide contains the description of the Thick Fleshy Starfish, including distinguishing features, colour, size, and distribution.<SEP>Page 430 of the document doc_sa_invertebrates_p0429_0432, which contains the taxonomic table and descriptive text for Chondraster elattosis.<SEP>Page 430 of the document, containing the Distinguishing Features Section and reference to Phylum Echinodermata.

Distinguishing Features Section (Page 429)
A section on page 429 that describes the distinguishing features of Luidia africana, such as fragile arms and spines.

Distinguishing Features Section (Page 430)
A section on page 430 that describes the distinguishing features of the Thick Fleshy Starfish, including rigid disc, madreporite, and tube feet.

Colour Section (Page 429)
A section on page 429 that states the colour of Luidia africana as brown to dark pink.

Colour Section (Page 430)
A section on page 430 that states the colour of the Thick Fleshy Starfish as bright pink to orange aborally and pale yellow orally.

Size Section (Page 429)
A section on page 429 that states the size of Luidia africana averages up to 150 mm diameter.

Size Section (Page 430)
A section on page 430 that states the size of the Thick Fleshy Starfish can reach up to 230 mm diameter.

Distribution Section (Page 429)
A section on page 429 that describes the distribution of Luidia africana: Southern African endemic, West and South Coasts to Port Elizabeth, 54-360+ m depth.

Distribution Section (Page 430)
A section on page 430 that describes the distribution of the Thick Fleshy Starfish: South African endemic, West and South Coasts of South Africa.

data.entity_name: Luidia sarsii africana taxonomic classification table (table)
data.content: This table provides the full taxonomic hierarchy for Luidia sarsii africana (Legs break easily starfish), from Phylum Echinodermata through Class Asteroidea, Order Paxillosida, Family Luidiidae, Genus Luidia, to Species sarsii africana. Its common name directly reflects the species' fragile arms as described in the surrounding text. The classification supports identification and contextualizes the species within the guide to South African offshore marine invertebrates.

data.entity_name: Poraniopsis echinaster (Spiky cushion star) Taxonomic Classification Table (table)
data.content: This table presents the full taxonomic hierarchy of Poraniopsis echinaster (Spiky cushion star), from phylum Echinodermata to species level. It complements the species description on page 432 by providing formal classification, reinforcing identification, and connecting morphological traits (e.g., spiky spines, cushion shape) with its scientific name. The table is sourced from a field guide and references authoritative works, serving as a quick reference for the organism's biological lineage.

data.entity_name: Field Guide to SA Offshore Marine Invertebrates
data.content:
Field Guide to SA Offshore Marine Invertebrates
Document containing page 429 with distinguishing features and image of the organism.<SEP>The Field Guide to SA Offshore Marine Invertebrates is a document containing images, sections, and references about marine invertebrates.<SEP>A field guide document (source filename: Field-Guide-to-SA-Offshore-Marine-Invertebrates_web-full-version_compressed.pdf) covering South African offshore marine invertebrates, containing pages 429-430 with descriptions and images.<SEP>A field guide document covering South African offshore marine invertebrates, including images and descriptions.

Echinodermata Distinguishing Anatomical Features
A set of distinguishing features including short-armed stellate body, reticular skeleton, raised spines (1-4mm), rigid arms, two rows of tube feet, white madreporite off-centre, and strong spines at arm base.

Image of Echinodermata Specimen
An image in the field guide showing a specimen of Phylum Echinodermata, with associated visual analysis and footnote metadata.

Luidia Sarsii Africana
A subspecies or variety of starfish in the family Luidiidae, known by the common name "Legs break easily starfish" and characterized by arms that break off easily from the central disc.

Asteroidea
Class to which Luidia sarsii africana belongs.<SEP>Asteroidea is the class to which Chondraster elattosis belongs.<SEP>Asteroidea is the class (starfish) to which Spoladaster veneris belongs.

Paxillosida
Order to which Luidia sarsii africana belongs.

Luidiidae
Family to which Luidia sarsii africana belongs.

Luidia
Genus to which Luidia sarsii africana belongs.

Legs Break Easily Starfish
Common name for Luidia sarsii africana, referencing its fragile arms.

Arms Break Off Easily
A characteristic behavior of Luidia sarsii africana where the arms easily detach from the central disc.

Image Of Luidia Sarsii Africana
An image with caption "Luidia sarsii africana (LucAfr)" located at the file path F:\rag\AquaBio-AgentRAG\data\mrag\raganything\parser_output\sa_invertebrates\sa_invertebrates_p0429_0432\windows_cli_recovery\sa_invertebrates_p0429_0432\txt\images\bc6529f0695c20bdffa198a972c385768b8bd607c593bb4d3ec1a13cb2f8c2f8.jpg.

data.entity_name: Taxonomic Classification Table For Luidia Sarsii Africana
data.content:
Taxonomic Classification Table For Luidia Sarsii Africana
A two-column taxonomic classification table with 7 rows covering Phylum, Class, Order, Family, Genus, Species, and Common name for Luidia sarsii africana.

Document Sa Invertebrates P0429 0432
A document with id doc_sa_invertebrates_p0429_0432, page 429, sourced from Field-Guide-to-SA-Offshore-Marine-Invertebrates_web-full-version_compressed.pdf, containing the table and species description.<SEP>Document with doc_id=doc_sa_invertebrates_p0429_0432, page=432, source=Field-Guide-to-SA-Offshore-Marine-Invertebrates_web-full-version_compressed.pdf.

Arm
An appendage of Luidia sarsii africana that is prone to breaking off from the central disc.

Field-Guide-to-SA-Offshore-Marine-Invertebrates_web-full-version_compressed.pdf
The PDF source document containing page 430 with the taxonomic table and descriptive text for Chondraster elattosis.<SEP>A PDF document containing a field guide to South African offshore marine invertebrates, including images and descriptions.<SEP>This is the source document containing the image and description of marine invertebrates.<SEP>A field guide to South African offshore marine invertebrates in compressed PDF format, containing images and descriptions.<SEP>A field guide document about South African offshore marine invertebrates, containing images, distribution, and species descriptions.

Image of Inflexible Rigid Starfish
An image of the thick fleshy starfish, with file path 7c1b1883e584bb2ac456ace483fbb53c535f7f494b863c6264c53aac96a58d7d.jpg.

Inflexible Rigid Starfish
A thick fleshy starfish with an inflexible, rigid star shape, pentagonal disc, indistinct marginal plates, distinct madreporite, and fine raised bumps along arms. It has smooth aboral and oral surfaces and double rows of tube feet.

Disc
Thick, solid, spongy disc of the Inflexible Rigid Star, pentagonal in shape.<SEP>Thick, solid, spongy, and pentagonal in shape.

Adambulacral Spines
Fine raised bumps (sheaths of adambulacral spines) forming distinct rows along each arm of the Inflexible Rigid Star; no spines apparent.<SEP>Fine raised bumps (sheaths of adambulacral spines) form distinct rows along each arm.

Brooding
Brooding behavior of the Inflexible Rigid Star, indicated by very distinct patterning on the aboral surface.<SEP>The starfish exhibits brooding behavior, during which the aboral surface patterning becomes very distinct.

Poraniopsis echinaster
Poraniopsis echinaster is a species of starfish commonly known as the Spiky cushion star, belonging to family Poraniidae, with a short-armed stellate body, reticular skeleton, raised spines 1–4 mm, color ranging from deep orange to red or white, and size averaging 50 mm up to 160 mm diameter.

Asteriodea
Asteriodea is the class (misspelling of Asteroidea) to which Poraniopsis echinaster belongs.

Valvatida
Valvatida is the order to which Chondraster elattosis belongs.<SEP>Valvatida is the order to which Spoladaster veneris belongs.<SEP>Valvatida is the order to which Poraniopsis echinaster belongs.

Poraniidae
Poraniidae is the family to which Chondraster elattosis belongs.<SEP>Poraniidae is the family to which Spoladaster veneris belongs.<SEP>Poraniidae is the family to which Poraniopsis echinaster belongs, containing cushion-like starfish.

Poraniopsis
Poraniopsis is the genus to which Poraniopsis echinaster belongs.

data.entity_name: Taxonomic Classification Table for Poraniopsis Echinaster
data.content:
Taxonomic Classification Table for Poraniopsis Echinaster
A table presenting the taxonomic classification for Poraniopsis echinaster, listing phylum, class, order, family, genus, species, and common name as a two-column key-value list.

Image of Poraniopsis Echinaster
Image with path F:\..\9c2f6d75a6269198747239e0a8c8c04a708d865f88fd90532b99a8661256146b.jpg and caption "Poraniopsis echinaster (PorEch)".

Raised Spines
Raised spines 1–4 mm in length are a distinguishing anatomical feature of Poraniopsis echinaster.<SEP>Distinct raised spines covering the aboral surface, 1-4 mm in length.

Stellate Body
Stellate body shape is a characteristic anatomical feature of Poraniopsis echinaster.

South Atlantic
The South Atlantic is a distribution area for Poraniopsis echinaster.

Lophaster Quadrispinus
Lophaster quadrispinus is a similar species to Poraniopsis echinaster.

Diplopteraster Multipes
Diplopteraster multipes is a similar species to Poraniopsis echinaster.

Unidentified Brittle Star
An unidentified marine invertebrate with distinguishing features including fragile arms that break off easily, distinct spines on the aboral margin, arms long, flexible, flattened, tapering, strap-like, and usually five arms.

It contains the image on page 429.<SEP>The document is a field guide covering offshore marine invertebrates of South Africa, from which the image and text are extracted.

Field-Guide-To-SA-Offshore-Marine-Invertebrates_Web-Full-Version_Compressed.pdf
A field guide document about South African offshore marine invertebrates.

Pentagonal Shape
The organism possesses a pentagonal shape as observed in the image and described in the distinguishing features.<SEP>The organism possesses a pentagonal shape.

Papillae
Numerous papillae coat the aboral surface of the organism.

Ventral Surface
The lower surface of the organism, smooth with fine lines.<SEP>The ventral surface is smooth with fine lines.

data.entity_name: South Atlantic Including West Coast Of South Africa
data.content:
South Atlantic Including West Coast Of South Africa
The geographic distribution area for the species discussed in the document, covering the South Atlantic and the west coast of South Africa.

Raised Tubercles
Dense raised tubercles are small rounded protuberances located on the aboral surface of Lophaster quadrispinus.

Echinodermata Distinguishing Features Image
An image file located at path '...51fcea3c5cdfa38f0626fbcf0289c22470c6f27f996a76ee5be9b003b7e5a90a.jpg' on page 429, with no captions, likely depicting distinguishing features of Echinodermata.

References Section (Page 429)
The references section on page 429 of the field guide contains citations and neighbor text about starfishes and phylum Echinodermata.

Image of Starfishes Reference
An image located on page 429 of the field guide, associated with the references section and neighbor text referencing Clark and Downey (1992) and Phylum Echinodermata.

Starfishes of the Atlantic (Volume 3)
A book by Clark AM and Downey ME (1992) about starfishes of the Atlantic, referenced in the neighbor text.

Inflexible Rigid Star
An inflexible, rigid star with a thick, solid, spongy disc, pentagonal shape, indistinct marginal plates, distinct madreporite, fine raised bumps (sheaths of adambulacral spines) forming distinct rows along each arm, no spines, thick fleshy with smooth aboral and oral surface, double rows of tube feet, and distinct patterning on the aboral surface when brooding.

Inflexible Rigid Star Image
An image of the Inflexible Rigid Star, located at path F:\rag\AquaBio-AgentRAG\data\mrag\raganything\parser_output\sa_invertebrates\sa_invertebrates_p0429_0432\windows_cli_recovery\sa_invertebrates_p0429_0432\txt\images\ce4f6c76e430f719134899606d5ce7546ca9b0eed312758e3312d7133969e480.jpg, with footnote referencing page 430 of the field guide.

SpoBra
SpoBra is the code name for the species Spoladaster veneris, used in the taxonomic table.

Spoladaster
Spoladaster is the genus containing species such as Spoladaster veneris and Spoladaster brachyactis.

Veneris
Veneris is the species epithet for Spoladaster veneris.

Inflated Star
Inflated Star is the common name for Spoladaster veneris, reflecting its trait of inflation.

Chondraster Ellatosis
Chondraster ellatosis is a starfish species that does not inflate and is more leathery compared with Spoladaster veneris.

Taxonomic Classification Table
The table is a taxonomic classification table for Spoladaster veneris showing ranks from Phylum to Species and the common name. It is depicted in an image file.

data.content:
Image of Invertebrate Arms and Spines
An image on page 429 illustrating the distinguishing features of an invertebrate, specifically arms and spines.

Chondraster elattosis
Chondraster elattosis is a starfish species known as Pentagon star, belonging to the family Poraniidae, with pentagonal body shape, cushion-like body, bright pink to orange aboral color, size up to 230 mm diameter, endemic to South Africa's West and South Coasts at depths 400 to 1000+ m.

Chondraster
Chondraster is the genus to which Chondraster elattosis belongs.

elattosis
elattosis is the species epithet for Chondraster elattosis.

Pentagon star
Pentagon star is the common name for Chondraster elattosis, referring to its pentagonal shape.

data.entity_name: Taxonomic classification table for Chondraster elattosis
data.content:
Taxonomic classification table for Chondraster elattosis
A table listing the taxonomic ranks from Phylum to Common name for Chondraster elattosis, with values Echinodermata, Asteroidea, Valvatida, Poraniidae, Chondraster, elattosis, Pentagon star.

Chondraster elattosis (ChoEla)
An image depicting Chondraster elattosis with caption "Chondraster elattosis (ChoEla)".

doc_sa_invertebrates_p0429_0432
A field guide document on South African offshore marine invertebrates, containing page 430 with information on Chondraster elattosis.

Pentagonal body shape
The pentagonal body shape of Chondraster elattosis, which gives it the common name Pentagon star.

Cushion-like body
The cushion-like body of Chondraster elattosis, a distinguishing morphological feature.

data.entity_name: Bright pink to orange aboral color
data.content:
Bright pink to orange aboral color
The bright pink to orange coloration on the aboral surface of Chondraster elattosis.

Size up to 230 mm diameter
The maximum size of Chondraster elattosis is up to 230 mm in diameter.

data.entity_name: South African West and South Coasts
data.content:
South African West and South Coasts
The geographic distribution of Chondraster elattosis is endemic to the West and South Coasts of South Africa.

Depth 400 to 1000+ m
The depth range of Chondraster elattosis is from 400 to over 1000 meters.

Spoladaster veneris
Spoladaster veneris is a similar species to Chondraster elattosis.

Pentagon Star
Pentagon Star is the common name for Chondraster elattosis, referring to its pentagonal shape.

e74ee4b8c4c6a339abfe8f79af7a783a8bdb662295217a306c3b7483962ff05a.jpg
An image of a marine invertebrate with speckled brilliant orange aboral surface and pale cream smooth oral surface.

Speckled Brilliant Orange Aboral Surface
The upper (aboral) surface of the invertebrate is speckled with brilliant orange coloration.

Pale Cream Smooth Oral Surface
The lower (oral) surface of the invertebrate is pale cream and smooth.

Depth From 40 To 205+ M
The invertebrate inhabits depths ranging from 40 to over 205 meters.

Pentagonal Cushion-Like Echinoderm Image
An image of a pentagonal, cushion-like echinoderm with numerous papillae on the aboral surface and a smooth ventral surface with fine lines, shown in an inflated state.

Fine Lines
Fine lines present on the ventral surface of the organism.

Inflation And Deflation Behavior
The organism is often inflated when landed but slowly deflates over time out of water.

Washington
Washington is the location of the Smithsonian institution.
